notes: According to the Sages, this convocation occurred on the morrow of the Day of Atonement, when Moses descended Mount Sinai bearing the tablets, having obtained divine pardon for Israel. (Nahum Sarna, The JPS Torah Commentary: Exodus, Philadelphia, 1989, 222)

spent the day in darkness, although some later adherents did accept the rabbinic practice. It was probably to demonstrate opposition to the early Karaite view that the kindling of lights on the eve of Sabbath gradually became obligatory. To this end, the geonim, the post-Talmudic heads of the Babylonian academies, instituted the recital of a blessing over them. (Nahum Sarna, The JPS Torah Commentary: Exodus, Philadelphia, 1989, 222)

2-3. The injunction is practically a verbatim repetition of 31:15, with an addition. The manner in which the prohibition against kindling fire on the Sabbath is worded led the rabbis of the Talmud to understand that fire may not be kindled on the Sabbath itself; however, fire lit before the Sabbath and not refueled on the Sabbath is permitted. The Jewish sectarians known as Karaites rejected this interpretation and

4-9. Moses issues a call for donations of materials in accordance with 25:1-9. He specifies the various materials and explains how they are to be used. The actual goal of constructing the Tabernacle is not explicitly stated, as in 25:8; it is assumed that the audience is already aware of the project. (Nahum Sarna, The JPS Torah Commentary: Exodus, Philadelphia, 1989, 222-223)
offering (teruwmah) an offering to God from (ruwm) to rise, rise up, be high, be lofty, be exalted - our offerings to the Lord exalt us.
willing (bydn nadiyb noble, generous) heart This is the foundation of a true offering.
badger (tachash) perhaps the badger or dugong (a river mammal like a walrus), dolphin, or sheep or a now extinct animal

the curtain for the screen The workmanship and function of the curtain, which partitions off the Holy of Holies from the Holy Place, is described in 26:31--33.3 In Numbers 4:s it is explained that at the breaking of camp during the trek through the wilderness, Aaron and his sons would enter the Tabernacle, remove the screening curtain, and cover the Ark with it. (Nahum Sarna, The JPS Torah Commentary: Exodus, Philadelphia, 1989, 223)

the entrance screen The curtain that partitions off the Holy Place (the outer sanctum) from the outer court, as described in 26:36-37. (Nahum Sarna, The JPS Torah Commentary: Exodus, Philadelphia, 1989, 223)

The people-men and women alike-respond to Moses' call with unstinting generosity, freely contributing their most precious possessions as well as their skilled services. (Nahum Sarna, The JPS Torah Commentary: Exodus, Philadelphia, 1989, 223)

The people are informed of God's designation of Bezalel and Oholiab as the chief artisans and supervisors of the entire project, as recorded in 31:1-11. Berakhot 55a remarks that this public announcement by Moses is to teach that one must not appoint a communal leader without first consulting the people. (Nahum Sarna, The JPS Torah Commentary: Exodus, Philadelphia, 1989, 224)

Just as the spirit of God moves about the waters of creation, it also moves through the workman Bezaleel: "the human artist is a craftsman who is filled with the 'spirit of God' (Ex. 35:31), the same rûah 'elohîm mentioned in Genesis 1:2 as moving over the waters of the primordial world of creation." Note that the spirit of God is also present at Nephi's construction of his work of curious workmanship (1 Nephi 17:52). (Boats, Beginnings, and Repetitions Alan Goff, FARMS Journal of Book of Mormon Studies, 1:79.) The tabernacle, like other divinely directed work projects, is done under the Lord's direction.

quotes.text: God moves about the waters of creation, it also moves through the workman Bezaleel: "the human artist is a craftsman who is filled with the 'spirit of God' (Ex. 35:31), the same rûah 'elohîm mentioned in Genesis 1:2 as moving over the waters of the primordial world of creation." Note that the spirit of God is also present at Nephi's construction of his work of curious workmanship (1 Nephi 17:52).

notes: They are endowed with the spiritual gift of teaching others (see D&C 46:12).
